perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed in that state, without regard to any of its principles of conflicts of laws or other laws that would result in the application of the laws of another jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted without regard to any presumption against the party causing this Agreement to be drafted. Each of the parties hereby unconditionally and irrevocably waives the right to a trial by jury in any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby. Each of the parties unconditionally and irrevocably consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of New York located in the County of New York and the Federal district court for the Southern District of New York located in the County of New York with respect to any suit, action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby, and each of the parties hereby unconditionally and irrevocably waives any objection to venue in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed in that state, without regard to any of its principles of conflicts of laws or other laws that would result in the application of the laws of another jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted without regard to any presumption against the party causing this Agreement to be drafted.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the jury trial waiver to a less prominent section can reduce its enforceability. Parties might overlook it, leading to potential challenges in court.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 7th Amendment to the United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The Seventh Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While this right can be waived, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. Obscuring the waiver clause within general provisions undermines the voluntariness of the waiver, potentially conflicting with the spirit and intent of the Seventh Amendment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The original contract text contains a jury trial waiver. The changed text removes this waiver, potentially violating the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial, as parties might inadvertently lose this right. The Law Explanation states that obscuring the waiver undermines its voluntariness, potentially conflicting with the Seventh Amendment. The Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms the existence of the right to a jury trial and that it can be waived. By removing the explicit waiver, the changed contract introduces a structural flaw that could lead to a legal contradiction, making the waiver less enforceable because it is no longer explicitly stated.

perturbation.original_text: If any action for breach of or to enforce the provisions of this Agreement is commenced, the court in such action shall award to the party in whose favor a judgment is entered, a reasonable sum as attorneys' fees and costs. Such attorneys' fees and costs shall be paid by the non-prevailing party in such action.
perturbation.changed_text: If any action for breach of or to enforce the provisions of this Agreement is commenced, the court in such action shall award to the party in whose favor a judgment is entered, a reasonable sum as attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the part that the attorneys' fees and costs shall be paid by the non-prevailing party in such action makes the section unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 8101
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 8101 dictates who receives the costs. Removing the specification of the fees to be paid by the non-prevailing party introduces ambiguity and could make the enforcement of fee recovery more difficult. The attorney fee clause is now incomplete and unenforceable.

Costs in an action. The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states that attorneys' fees and costs shall be paid by the non-prevailing party. The changed text removes this obligation, creating ambiguity regarding who is responsible for these fees. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CVP CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 81 § 8101, states that 'The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action.' While this snippet doesn't explicitly mandate the non-prevailing party to pay, removing the specification from the original contract undermines the enforceability of recovering attorney's fees, creating a structural flaw that introduces a legal contradiction because it makes the application of costs ambiguous and potentially unenforceable as intended.

perturbation.original_text: The provisions of this Agreement shall be deemed severable and if any portion hereof shall be held invalid, illegal or unenforceable for any reason by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remainder shall not thereby be invalidated but shall remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: The provisions of this Agreement shall be deemed severable and if any portion hereof shall be held invalid, illegal or unenforceable for any reason by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remainder shall not thereby be invalidated.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the portion regarding the enforceability makes the contract subject to multiple interpretations, since the contract doesn't specifically spell out what will happen if the court does not act in accordance with the severability, leading to a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 184 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts addresses the effect of severability clauses. Removing the phrase 'but shall remain in full force and effect' contradicts the standard interpretation of severability, making it unclear whether the remaining provisions are still enforceable if a part of the contract is deemed invalid.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures that if a portion of the agreement is deemed invalid, illegal, or unenforceable, the remaining portions still remain in full force and effect. The changed text removes 'but shall remain in full force and effect,' creating ambiguity about the enforceability of the remaining provisions if a part is deemed invalid by a court. The Law Explanation confirms this, stating it contradicts the standard interpretation of severability. While Scraped Law Snippets are unreadable, the Law Explanation indicates that Section 184 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts addresses the effect of severability clauses, and removing this phrase contradicts that standard interpretation. Therefore, the modification weakens the severability clause, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the restrictive covenants contained in Section 1 and/or Section 2 of this Agreement shall be found by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unreasonable by reason of such restrictive covenants extending for too great a period of time or over too great a geographic area or by reason of such restrictive covenants being too extensive in any other respect, then such restrictive covenant shall be deemed modified to the minimum extent necessary to make such restrictive covenant reasonable and enforceable under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the restrictive covenants contained in Section 1 and/or Section 2 of this Agreement shall be found by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unreasonable by reason of such restrictive covenants extending for too great a period of time or over too great a geographic area or by reason of such restrictive covenants being too extensive in any other respect, then such restrictive covenant shall be deemed modified to the minimum extent necessary to make such restrictive covenant reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: The 'Blue Pencil Doctrine' section is purposefully rendered incomplete. By stopping mid-sentence, the intention of modification to make the covenant enforceable is cut off. This creates doubt as to the court's role.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Jurisprudence 2d, Contracts § 194, discusses the 'blue pencil' doctrine, which allows courts to strike out unenforceable portions of a contract to make the remaining parts enforceable. By removing the words, the intent and mechanism are incomplete.

perturbation.justification: The altered text creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. The original text's clause on restrictive covenants includes the phrase 'enforceable under the circumstances,' which provides a clear legal endpoint to the modification process according to the 'blue pencil' doctrine. The changed text removes this enforceability clause, creating ambiguity as to the ultimate goal of the modification, and leaving the court's role incomplete. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the removal of words makes the mechanism incomplete.
